meta.title: The Los Angeles Aqueduct is Wild
meta.author: Practical Engineering

questions.question: In engineering usage, an “aqueduct” refers to what?
options: Only a bridge carrying water
Any long-distance system conveying water to a distribution point
A reservoir used to store water
A tunnel exclusively used for sewerage
questions.answer: Any long-distance system conveying water to a distribution point

questions.question: What was the “official start” of the LA Aqueduct (as described in the video’s tour)?
options: The Los Angeles Reservoir
The Owens River Diversion Weir
Fairmont Reservoir
Elizabeth Tunnel
questions.answer: The Owens River Diversion Weir

questions.question: How does the LA Aqueduct primarily move water toward Los Angeles?
options: With continuous pumping
By gravity-driven flow with elevation management
By wind-powered turbines
By desalination and chemical treatment
questions.answer: By gravity-driven flow with elevation management

questions.question: Why is maintaining a relatively constant slope important for the open canal section?
options: It reduces the need for electricity generation
It improves the efficiency of flow
It prevents the canal from being vandalized
It ensures the water arrives in Los Angeles instantly
questions.answer: It improves the efficiency of flow

questions.question: Roughly how long is the water journey the aqueduct system supports (as stated for the overall trip)?
options: About 30 miles
About 100 miles
About 300 miles
About 1,000 miles
questions.answer: About 300 miles

questions.question: Which statement best describes the political conflict around the Owens Valley acquisitions?
options: Acquisitions were portrayed as transparent and cooperative
Acquisitions were carried out in bad faith, straining relationships and disrupting local life
Local residents had little involvement in opposition
The conflict was limited to disputes over bridge design
questions.answer: Acquisitions were carried out in bad faith, straining relationships and disrupting local life

questions.question: What is one example of resistance described by the video from the early 1920s?
options: Residents used paint to mark the aqueduct
Ranchers used dynamite to blow up a part of the canal
A court ordered the gates to be permanently welded shut
The aqueduct was moved underground immediately after construction began
questions.answer: Ranchers used dynamite to blow up a part of the canal

questions.question: What do the Alabama Gates do in the aqueduct system?
options: They permanently close the canal to stop all flow
They act as a spillway allowing operators to divert water back to the Owens River
They filter water before it reaches Los Angeles
They store water to be released later for irrigation
questions.answer: They act as a spillway allowing operators to divert water back to the Owens River

questions.question: Why was the Alabama Gates area an important dividing line in design (as explained)?
options: It marked where pumps were introduced to maintain pressure
It marked a transition from an unlined canal to a concrete-lined channel to reduce seepage losses
It marked where the system became desalinated
It marked where the hydropower plants were first installed
questions.answer: It marked a transition from an unlined canal to a concrete-lined channel to reduce seepage losses

questions.question: What problem emerges later from Owens Lake’s drying (beyond immediate ecological impacts)?
options: The lakebed becomes too cold for wildlife
Exposed sediment becomes airborne dust that is dangerous to breathe
Owens Lake turns into a saline ocean
The water changes color and becomes unusable for drinking
questions.answer: Exposed sediment becomes airborne dust that is dangerous to breathe

questions.question: According to the video, what did LA spend to address Owens Lake dust pollution?
options: Less than $10 million
About $100 million
More than a billion dollars
About $10 billion
questions.answer: More than a billion dollars

questions.question: Why was Haiwee Reservoir originally built?
options: To replace the need for diversion structures
As a buffer so the aqueduct could keep running during upstream disruptions
To eliminate the need for filtration in Los Angeles
To increase the aqueduct’s slope using mechanical leveling
questions.answer: As a buffer so the aqueduct could keep running during upstream disruptions

questions.question: In the 1960s, how was the reservoir configuration changed, and what did that enable conceptually?
options: It was reduced to a single basin, simplifying operations
It was reconfigured into two basins to add flexibility as the aqueduct became two
It was moved to a coastal location
It was converted into underground storage with no surface exposure
questions.answer: It was reconfigured into two basins to add flexibility as the aqueduct became two

questions.question: What additional infrastructure was built in 1970 (as described) to increase flow capacity?
options: A new diversion weir
A second aqueduct/conduit
A replacement tunnel for Elizabeth
A pumping station for the open canal section
questions.answer: A second aqueduct/conduit

questions.question: Which set of benefits does the video associate with running aqueduct sections underground in a desert landscape?
options: More evaporation and more contamination risk
Less evaporation and reduced contamination risk, plus fewer surface ecological disruptions
More frequent wildlife deaths due to surface barriers
A requirement for continuous pumping
questions.answer: Less evaporation and reduced contamination risk, plus fewer surface ecological disruptions

questions.question: What is the trade-off the video emphasizes when comparing underground conduits to surface channels?
options: Underground conduits are cheaper
Underground conduits eliminate all topographic difficulties
Underground conduits are more expensive than simple surface channels
Surface channels eliminate seepage issues completely
questions.answer: Underground conduits are more expensive than simple surface channels

questions.question: What structure type is used at Jawbone Canyon to handle a major drop without a large detour?
options: A gravity siphon with open exposure
An inverted siphon with pressurized flow
A floating pipeline
A series of pumps mounted aboveground
questions.answer: An inverted siphon with pressurized flow

questions.question: The video states that 850 feet of water column corresponds to roughly what pressure?
options: About 40 psi
About 120 psi
About 370 psi (around 26 bar)
About 1,000 psi
questions.answer: About 370 psi (around 26 bar)

questions.question: How were the specially manufactured steel components for large pressurized siphon sections transported (as described)?
options: By rail directly across the continent
By ship around Cape Horn because the Panama Canal was under construction
By pipeline through the Sierra tunnel
By air freight only
questions.answer: By ship around Cape Horn because the Panama Canal was under construction

questions.question: What is the role of the newly completed transfer facility between the California Aqueduct and the LA Aqueduct (at the crossing)?
options: It permanently disconnects LA from the state system
It can pump water directly into the first LA aqueduct, enabling flexibility in trades
It filters the state’s water before it reaches any LA treatment
It stores water only during earthquakes, not during normal operations
questions.answer: It can pump water directly into the first LA aqueduct, enabling flexibility in trades

questions.question: Why is the second (later) aqueduct project described as taking a more direct path than the earlier one?
options: Because it avoided using any specialized materials
Because labor costs rose more than material costs, making pressurized conduit materials more acceptable
Because the earlier project followed topography only by mistake
Because later engineers refused to use concrete lining
questions.answer: Because labor costs rose more than material costs, making pressurized conduit materials more acceptable

questions.question: Where do the two aqueducts come back together (as stated after more than a hundred miles apart)?
options: At Drinkwater Reservoir
At Fairmont Reservoir
At Los Angeles Reservoir
At Owens Lake’s dried lakebed
questions.answer: At Fairmont Reservoir

questions.question: What did Elizabeth Tunnel primarily enable for the aqueduct system?
options: A detour to avoid hard rock
Crossing the mountain range without pumps by providing a major tunnel passage
An open-air canal section across the Sierra Pelona Mountains
A replacement for hydropower plants
questions.answer: Crossing the mountain range without pumps by providing a major tunnel passage

questions.question: What is one stated function of the hydroelectric plants along the aqueduct?
options: They only serve as decorative museum sites
They capture energy from water as it flows downward and help pay for parts of the project
They replace filtration and disinfection steps
They are used to desalinate water
questions.answer: They capture energy from water as it flows downward and help pay for parts of the project

questions.question: Which design element is described as providing “insurance” against simultaneous shortages during a major earthquake risk scenario?
options: A single-use temporary bridge
Bouquet Canyon reservoir
The Alabama Gates spillway mechanism
Plastic shade balls
questions.answer: Bouquet Canyon reservoir

questions.question: What disaster is described as having famously destroyed a reservoir-related project, affecting parts of the aqueduct system?
options: The St. Francis Dam failure (1928)
The Mono Lake dust storm of 1932
The California Aqueduct construction strike of 1945
The Owens Lake flooding of 1970
questions.answer: The St. Francis Dam failure (1928)

questions.question: After the St. Francis Dam failure, what does the video claim about restoration time for aqueduct service?
options: It took decades to restore
It restored to functioning service in about 12 days
It was never restored
Restoration took less than a day
questions.answer: It restored to functioning service in about 12 days

questions.question: What change in the 2000s did drinking-water rules drive regarding open reservoirs holding treated water?
options: They required replacing reservoirs with pumps
They pushed utilities to add stronger protection using floating plastic shade balls
They required removing all reservoir covers permanently
They eliminated the need for disinfection
questions.answer: They pushed utilities to add stronger protection using floating plastic shade balls

questions.question: How did LA extend its upstream water sourcing beyond the Owens River project (as described in 1940)?
options: By connecting to the Colorado River system in 1940
By connecting the Mono basin and funneling its water through tunnels to the Owens River basin
By drilling desalination wells in the Owens Valley
By building a new gravity canal from the Pacific coast
questions.answer: By connecting the Mono basin and funneling its water through tunnels to the Owens River basin

questions.question: What lesson does the video emphasize about engineering’s future role at the intersection of forces like geology, climate, culture, politics, and human need?
options: Engineering should avoid considering politics entirely
Engineering makes a difference by grappling with the messy intersection of multiple constraints
Engineering should treat landscapes as machines with only one output and ignore downstream effects
Engineering only matters when climate stays perfectly predictable forever
questions.answer: Engineering makes a difference by grappling with the messy intersection of multiple constraints
